texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Grave of the Slave
texts.text: The Grave of the Slave

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The cold storms of winter shall chill him no more, His woes and his sorrows, his pains are all o'er; The sod of the valley now covers his form, He is safe in his last home, he feels not the storm.
texts.text: The cold storms of winter shall chill him no more, His woes and his sorrows, his pains are all o'er; The sod of the valley now covers his form, He is safe in his last home, he feels not the storm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The poor slave is laid all unheeded and lone, Where the rich and the poor find a permanent home; Not his master can rouse him with voice of command; He knows not, he hears not, his cruel demand.
texts.text: The poor slave is laid all unheeded and lone, Where the rich and the poor find a permanent home; Not his master can rouse him with voice of command; He knows not, he hears not, his cruel demand.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Not a tear, not a sigh to embalm his cold tomb, No friend to lament him, no child to bemoan; Not a stone marks the place, where he peacefully lies, The earth for his pillow, his curtain the skies.
texts.text: Not a tear, not a sigh to embalm his cold tomb, No friend to lament him, no child to bemoan; Not a stone marks the place, where he peacefully lies, The earth for his pillow, his curtain the skies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poor slave! shall we sorrow that death was thy friend, The last, and the kindest, that heaven could send? The grave to the weary is welcomed and blest; And death, to the captive, is freedom and rest.
texts.text: Poor slave! shall we sorrow that death was thy friend, The last, and the kindest, that heaven could send? The grave to the weary is welcomed and blest; And death, to the captive, is freedom and rest.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Slave Girl’s Address to Her Mother
texts.text: The Slave Girl's Address to Her Mother

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh! mother, weep not, though our lot be hard, And we are helpless-God will be our guard: For He our heavenly guardian doth not sleep; He watches o'er us-mother, do not weep.
texts.text: Oh! mother, weep not, though our lot be hard, And we are helpless-God will be our guard: For He our heavenly guardian doth not sleep; He watches o'er us-mother, do not weep.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And grieve not for that dear loved home no more; Our sufferings and our wrongs, ah! why deplore? For though we feel the stern oppressor's rod, Yet he must yield, as well as we, to God.
texts.text: And grieve not for that dear loved home no more; Our sufferings and our wrongs, ah! why deplore? For though we feel the stern oppressor's rod, Yet he must yield, as well as we, to God.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Torn from our home, our kindred and our friends, And in a stranger's land our days to end, No heart feels for the poor, the bleeding slave; No arm is stretched to rescue, and to save.
texts.text: Torn from our home, our kindred and our friends, And in a stranger's land our days to end, No heart feels for the poor, the bleeding slave; No arm is stretched to rescue, and to save.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh! ye who boast of Freedom's sacred claims, Do ye not blush to see our galling chains; To hear that sounding word-"that all are free"- When thousands groan in hopeless
texts.text: Oh! ye who boast of Freedom's sacred claims, Do ye not blush to see our galling chains; To hear that sounding word-"that all are free"- When thousands groan in hopeless

texts.label: text
texts.orig: slavery
texts.text: slavery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upon your land it is a cruel stain- Freedom, what art thou?-nothing but a name. No more, no more! Oh God, this cannot be; Thou to thy children's aid wilt surely flee: In thine own time deliverance thou wilt give, And bid us rise from slavery, and live.
texts.text: Upon your land it is a cruel stain- Freedom, what art thou?-nothing but a name. No more, no more! Oh God, this cannot be; Thou to thy children's aid wilt surely flee: In thine own time deliverance thou wilt give, And bid us rise from slavery, and live.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Past Joys
texts.text: Past Joys

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The friends we've loved, the home we've left, Will ofttimes claim a tear; And though of these we are bereft, Still memory makes them dear.
texts.text: The friends we've loved, the home we've left, Will ofttimes claim a tear; And though of these we are bereft, Still memory makes them dear.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And deep we feel each trifling ill, Each sorrow of the soul: But care we for the painful thrill, That o'er some breasts doth roll?
texts.text: And deep we feel each trifling ill, Each sorrow of the soul: But care we for the painful thrill, That o'er some breasts doth roll?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poor Afric's son-ah! he must feel How hard it is to part From all he lov'd-from all that life Had twined around his heart.
texts.text: Poor Afric's son-ah! he must feel How hard it is to part From all he lov'd-from all that life Had twined around his heart.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: His is a sorrow deeper far, Than all that we can show; His is a lasting grief, o'er which No healing balm can flow.
texts.text: His is a sorrow deeper far, Than all that we can show; His is a lasting grief, o'er which No healing balm can flow.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mother, wife, or child he loved, He ne'er shall see again; To him they're lost-ay, dead indeed: What for him doth remain?
texts.text: The mother, wife, or child he loved, He ne'er shall see again; To him they're lost-ay, dead indeed: What for him doth remain?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A feeling of deep wretchedness Comes o'er his troubled soul; The thoughts of home-of other days, In painful visions roll.
texts.text: A feeling of deep wretchedness Comes o'er his troubled soul; The thoughts of home-of other days, In painful visions roll.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: His home-ah! that lov'd name recalls All that was dear to him; But
texts.text: His home-ah! that lov'd name recalls All that was dear to him; But

texts.label: text
texts.orig: these
texts.text: these

texts.label: text
texts.orig: were scenes he'll know no more- He only
texts.text: were scenes he'll know no more- He only

texts.label: text
texts.orig: feels
texts.text: feels

texts.label: text
texts.orig: they've been.
texts.text: they've been.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Prayer
texts.text: Prayer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This sacred right none are denied, Which makes the soul to Christ allied; Man bends the heart and bows the knee, And knows in prayer that he is free.
texts.text: This sacred right none are denied, Which makes the soul to Christ allied; Man bends the heart and bows the knee, And knows in prayer that he is free.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yes, free to ask of Him, whate'er The fainting heart alone can cheer; To worship at that holy shrine, Where beams thy Spirit, Lord, divine.
texts.text: Yes, free to ask of Him, whate'er The fainting heart alone can cheer; To worship at that holy shrine, Where beams thy Spirit, Lord, divine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And Afric's children they are free, To breathe their vows, their prayers to Thee; With thought of future joy and gain, The slave forgets his grief and pain;
texts.text: And Afric's children they are free, To breathe their vows, their prayers to Thee; With thought of future joy and gain, The slave forgets his grief and pain;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Forgets awhile his slavish fear; Forgets-that fetters bind him here; And in that sweet communion rest His hopes, his fears-for he is blest.
texts.text: Forgets awhile his slavish fear; Forgets-that fetters bind him here; And in that sweet communion rest His hopes, his fears-for he is blest.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Slave
texts.text: The Slave

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our sires who once in freedom's cause, Their boasted freedom sought and won, For deeds of glory gained applause, When patriot feelings led them on. And can their sons now speak with pride, Of rights for which they bled and died- Or while the captive is oppressed, Think of the wrongs they once redress'd? Oh, surely they have quite forgot, That bondage once had been their lot; The sweets of freedom now they know, They care not for the captive's woe. The poor wronged slave can bear no part In feelings dearest to his heart; He cannot speak on freedom's side, Nor dare he own a freeman's pride, His soul is dark, ay dark as night, O'er which is shed no gleam of light; A cloud of error, doubt and fear, O'er him is ever hovering near; And sad and hard his lot must be, To know that he can ne'er be free; To feel that
texts.text: Our sires who once in freedom's cause, Their boasted freedom sought and won, For deeds of glory gained applause, When patriot feelings led them on. And can their sons now speak with pride, Of rights for which they bled and died- Or while the captive is oppressed, Think of the wrongs they once redress'd? Oh, surely they have quite forgot, That bondage once had been their lot; The sweets of freedom now they know, They care not for the captive's woe. The poor wronged slave can bear no part In feelings dearest to his heart; He cannot speak on freedom's side, Nor dare he own a freeman's pride, His soul is dark, ay dark as night, O'er which is shed no gleam of light; A cloud of error, doubt and fear, O'er him is ever hovering near; And sad and hard his lot must be, To know that he can ne'er be free; To feel that

texts.label: text
texts.orig: his
texts.text: his

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is doomed to be A life, and death, of slavery. But will not justice soon arise, And plead the cause of the despised? For oh! my country, must it be, That they still find a foe in thee?
texts.text: is doomed to be A life, and death, of slavery. But will not justice soon arise, And plead the cause of the despised? For oh! my country, must it be, That they still find a foe in thee?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Farewell
texts.text: The Farewell

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Farewell!-but thou wilt soon forget The stranger thou hast seen, And in the gay and busy world, Forget that I have been.
texts.text: Farewell!-but thou wilt soon forget The stranger thou hast seen, And in the gay and busy world, Forget that I have been.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And thought of me will scarce intrude, When other forms are nigh; Who, decked in beauty's bright array, Shall pass before thine eye.
texts.text: And thought of me will scarce intrude, When other forms are nigh; Who, decked in beauty's bright array, Shall pass before thine eye.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another's lips will charm thee then, Another's voice will praise; Thou wilt forget we e'er have met In past and happy days.
texts.text: Another's lips will charm thee then, Another's voice will praise; Thou wilt forget we e'er have met In past and happy days.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And thou wilt scarcely deign to think Of friendship's early dream, Or cast one glance, in after years, On this poor offering.
texts.text: And thou wilt scarcely deign to think Of friendship's early dream, Or cast one glance, in after years, On this poor offering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Farewell-farewell!-'twere better far That we had never met, Than meeting one brief moment here, To part-and then forget.
texts.text: Farewell-farewell!-'twere better far That we had never met, Than meeting one brief moment here, To part-and then forget.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A Mother’s Grief
texts.text: A Mother's Grief

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There lies the sole remaining hope Of all my coming years; The treasure of my widow'd heart, The tie that bound me here.
texts.text: There lies the sole remaining hope Of all my coming years; The treasure of my widow'd heart, The tie that bound me here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: He was the last-the loveliest, And can you blame my grief, Or chide the falling tears which give This bursting heart relief?
texts.text: He was the last-the loveliest, And can you blame my grief, Or chide the falling tears which give This bursting heart relief?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There's nothing left for me to love This earth holds nothing dear, Since
texts.text: There's nothing left for me to love This earth holds nothing dear, Since

texts.label: text
texts.orig: he
texts.text: he

texts.label: text
texts.orig: , my sweet-my gentle one, Is now no longer here.
texts.text: , my sweet-my gentle one, Is now no longer here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: My poor fond heart had counted on Such bliss, in future hours! And I had dreamed his coming days Here strewed with fairy flowers!
texts.text: My poor fond heart had counted on Such bliss, in future hours! And I had dreamed his coming days Here strewed with fairy flowers!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Perchance 'twas wrong to love him thus, And I have been chastised- For He who gave him to my trust, Hath called him to the skies.
texts.text: Perchance 'twas wrong to love him thus, And I have been chastised- For He who gave him to my trust, Hath called him to the skies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: He was too dear-oh! far too dear, The idol of my soul- Then blame me not-this burst of grief I cannot now control.
texts.text: He was too dear-oh! far too dear, The idol of my soul- Then blame me not-this burst of grief I cannot now control.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: To the Hibernia
texts.text: To the Hibernia

texts.label: text
texts.orig: O, speed thee! speed thee! gallant bark, Across the bounding wave; Thou bearest to old Britain's shores, The Champion of the slave.
texts.text: O, speed thee! speed thee! gallant bark, Across the bounding wave; Thou bearest to old Britain's shores, The Champion of the slave.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Propitious breezes waft thee on, Safe o'er Atlantic's sea; For many a heart with fervor sends A benizon to thee.
texts.text: Propitious breezes waft thee on, Safe o'er Atlantic's sea; For many a heart with fervor sends A benizon to thee.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And he who fears not to commit His body to thy care, Fears not to brave the winds and waves, Knowing that God is there.
texts.text: And he who fears not to commit His body to thy care, Fears not to brave the winds and waves, Knowing that God is there.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: He goes to raise the standard high, And freedom's flag unfurl, And to proclaim the rallying cry Of freedom to the world.
texts.text: He goes to raise the standard high, And freedom's flag unfurl, And to proclaim the rallying cry Of freedom to the world.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then swift and steady be thy flight, Across the briny wave; And safely bear, O noble bark, The Champion of the slave.
texts.text: Then swift and steady be thy flight, Across the briny wave; And safely bear, O noble bark, The Champion of the slave.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Life
texts.text: Life

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To see our pathway strewn with flowers, While 'neath their sunny hue, We fear that many a rankling care Lies hidden from our view;
texts.text: To see our pathway strewn with flowers, While 'neath their sunny hue, We fear that many a rankling care Lies hidden from our view;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To cull the flowers, to love their bloom, A season to enjoy; To mark the blast that rudely comes Their beauties to destroy:-
texts.text: To cull the flowers, to love their bloom, A season to enjoy; To mark the blast that rudely comes Their beauties to destroy:-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Such is our life. How full of change! How much of hope or fears! Scarce on the cheek is seen the smile, Ere it is lost in tears.
texts.text: Such is our life. How full of change! How much of hope or fears! Scarce on the cheek is seen the smile, Ere it is lost in tears.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To see the cherished things of earth, On which we placed the heart, Fade, one by one, before our eyes, And silently depart;-
texts.text: To see the cherished things of earth, On which we placed the heart, Fade, one by one, before our eyes, And silently depart;-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This is our life-and who can tell What future hours may bring? What hopes will cheer the panting heart, While close to earth we cling?
texts.text: This is our life-and who can tell What future hours may bring? What hopes will cheer the panting heart, While close to earth we cling?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The morning of our days are bright, And could the vision last- But 'tis forbid-oh! changeful life, How soon the dream is past!
texts.text: The morning of our days are bright, And could the vision last- But 'tis forbid-oh! changeful life, How soon the dream is past!

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Separation
texts.text: The Separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Friend after friend departs."
texts.text: "Friend after friend departs."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And they are gone-that little band Of friends-the firm and true! We feel the void which absence makes, With joy, and sorrow too.
texts.text: And they are gone-that little band Of friends-the firm and true! We feel the void which absence makes, With joy, and sorrow too.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We joy that duties call them forth, Clad in an armor bright; With shield of faith, their surest guard, And sword of truth and light.
texts.text: We joy that duties call them forth, Clad in an armor bright; With shield of faith, their surest guard, And sword of truth and light.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We bid God speed their parting steps, And bless the righteous cause:- Where'er the path of duty points, May duty never pause.
texts.text: We bid God speed their parting steps, And bless the righteous cause:- Where'er the path of duty points, May duty never pause.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And yet, we sorrow most of all, And from the heart deplore, That we perchance on earth again May see these friends no more.
texts.text: And yet, we sorrow most of all, And from the heart deplore, That we perchance on earth again May see these friends no more.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Their works shall live when other deeds, Which ask a nation's fame, Have sank beneath Time's whelming wave, Unhonored and unnamed.
texts.text: Their works shall live when other deeds, Which ask a nation's fame, Have sank beneath Time's whelming wave, Unhonored and unnamed.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: My Country
texts.text: My Country

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh! speak not of heathenish darkness again, Nor tell me of lands held in error's dread chain! Where-where is the nation so erring as we, Who claim the proud name of the "
texts.text: Oh! speak not of heathenish darkness again, Nor tell me of lands held in error's dread chain! Where-where is the nation so erring as we, Who claim the proud name of the "

texts.label: text
texts.orig: home of the free
texts.text: home of the free

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "! What a throb do the lov'd ties of country awake In the heart of the exile!-for time cannot break The sweet vision of home, and all he loved well, Which has thrown o'er his pathway a magical spell. Can the name of "
texts.text: "! What a throb do the lov'd ties of country awake In the heart of the exile!-for time cannot break The sweet vision of home, and all he loved well, Which has thrown o'er his pathway a magical spell. Can the name of "

texts.label: text
texts.orig: my country
texts.text: my country

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "-the deeds which we sing- Be honored-revered-'midst pollution and sin? Can the names of our fathers who perished in fight, Be hallowed in story, midst slavery's blight? When America's standard is floating so fair, I blush that the impress of falsehood is there; That oppression and mockery dim the high fame, That seeks from all nations a patriot's name. Speak not of "my country," unless she shall be, In truth, the bright home of the "brave and the free!" Till the dark stain of slavery is washed from her hand, A tribute of homage she cannot command.
texts.text: "-the deeds which we sing- Be honored-revered-'midst pollution and sin? Can the names of our fathers who perished in fight, Be hallowed in story, midst slavery's blight? When America's standard is floating so fair, I blush that the impress of falsehood is there; That oppression and mockery dim the high fame, That seeks from all nations a patriot's name. Speak not of "my country," unless she shall be, In truth, the bright home of the "brave and the free!" Till the dark stain of slavery is washed from her hand, A tribute of homage she cannot command.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Hours of Childhood
texts.text: Hours of Childhood

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Blest hours of childhood! then, and then alone, Dance we the revels gay round pleasure's throne."
texts.text: "Blest hours of childhood! then, and then alone, Dance we the revels gay round pleasure's throne."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dear cherished hours, how much ye tell Of all we've known, and loved so well! On memory's page there is a leaf, Bearing a trace of pleasures brief; Of schoolday mirth, of pastime gay, With which we whiled those hours away.
texts.text: Dear cherished hours, how much ye tell Of all we've known, and loved so well! On memory's page there is a leaf, Bearing a trace of pleasures brief; Of schoolday mirth, of pastime gay, With which we whiled those hours away.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And those we loved in early youth, With all the fervency of truth- They seem to live, and pass us by, With laughing lip and beaming eye. Each favorite spot, each winding stream, Where we have watched the bright sunbeam, Appears at memory's magic spell- And joys, to which we bid farewell.
texts.text: And those we loved in early youth, With all the fervency of truth- They seem to live, and pass us by, With laughing lip and beaming eye. Each favorite spot, each winding stream, Where we have watched the bright sunbeam, Appears at memory's magic spell- And joys, to which we bid farewell.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When grief and bliss are in the train Of hours we ne'er shall live again; When sorrow clouds our after years, And fills our cup with bitter tears; 'Tis then we wait a sigh to ye O by-gone days of memory.
texts.text: When grief and bliss are in the train Of hours we ne'er shall live again; When sorrow clouds our after years, And fills our cup with bitter tears; 'Tis then we wait a sigh to ye O by-gone days of memory.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: An Appeal to Woman
texts.text: An Appeal to Woman

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh, woman, woman, in thy brightest hour Of conscious worth, of pride, of conscious power, Oh, nobly dare to act a Christian's part, That well befits a lovely woman's heart! Dare to be good, as thou canst dare be great; Despise the taunts of envy, scorn and hate; Our "skins may differ," but from thee we claim A sister's privilege, in a sister's name.
texts.text: Oh, woman, woman, in thy brightest hour Of conscious worth, of pride, of conscious power, Oh, nobly dare to act a Christian's part, That well befits a lovely woman's heart! Dare to be good, as thou canst dare be great; Despise the taunts of envy, scorn and hate; Our "skins may differ," but from thee we claim A sister's privilege, in a sister's name.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are thy sisters-God has truly said, That of one blood, the nations he has made. Oh, Christian woman, in a Christian land, Canst thou unblushing read this great command? Suffer the wrongs which wring our inmost heart To draw one throb of pity on thy part; Our "skins may differ," but from thee we claim A sister's privilege, in a sister's name.
texts.text: We are thy sisters-God has truly said, That of one blood, the nations he has made. Oh, Christian woman, in a Christian land, Canst thou unblushing read this great command? Suffer the wrongs which wring our inmost heart To draw one throb of pity on thy part; Our "skins may differ," but from thee we claim A sister's privilege, in a sister's name.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh, woman!-though upon thy fairer brow The hues of roses and of lilies glow- These soon must wither in their kindred earth, From whence the fair and dark have equal birth. Let a bright halo o'er thy virtues shed A lustre, that shall live when thou art dead; Let coming ages learn to bless thy name Upon the altar of immortal fame.
texts.text: Oh, woman!-though upon thy fairer brow The hues of roses and of lilies glow- These soon must wither in their kindred earth, From whence the fair and dark have equal birth. Let a bright halo o'er thy virtues shed A lustre, that shall live when thou art dead; Let coming ages learn to bless thy name Upon the altar of immortal fame.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Slave Girl’s Farewell
texts.text: The Slave Girl's Farewell

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The incident which suggested the following lines is this:-A young girl was living with her mother in one of the West Indian Islands, quite unconscious of her being a slave. Her master, on leaving the Island for a permanent residence in Louisiana, cruelly separated the girl from her parent forever.
texts.text: The incident which suggested the following lines is this:-A young girl was living with her mother in one of the West Indian Islands, quite unconscious of her being a slave. Her master, on leaving the Island for a permanent residence in Louisiana, cruelly separated the girl from her parent forever.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mother, I leave thee-thou hast been, Through long, long years of pain, The only hope my fond heart knew; Or e'er shall know again.
texts.text: Mother, I leave thee-thou hast been, Through long, long years of pain, The only hope my fond heart knew; Or e'er shall know again.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sails are set-my master waits To bear me far from thee; I linger-can I give thee up, And cross the fearful sea?
texts.text: The sails are set-my master waits To bear me far from thee; I linger-can I give thee up, And cross the fearful sea?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oh, let me gaze! how bright it seems, As busy memory flies, To view those scenes of other days, Beneath those bright blue skies.
texts.text: Oh, let me gaze! how bright it seems, As busy memory flies, To view those scenes of other days, Beneath those bright blue skies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The little hut where I have played In childhood's fearless hours- The murmuring stream-the mossy bank, Where I have gathered flowers.
texts.text: The little hut where I have played In childhood's fearless hours- The murmuring stream-the mossy bank, Where I have gathered flowers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I knew not
texts.text: I knew not

texts.label: text
texts.orig: then
texts.text: then

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I was a slave, Or that another's will, Save thine, could bend my spirit's pride; Or bid my lips be still.
texts.text: I was a slave, Or that another's will, Save thine, could bend my spirit's pride; Or bid my lips be still.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Who now will soothe me at my toil, Or bathe my weary brow? Or shield me when the heavy lash Is raised to give the blow?
texts.text: Who now will soothe me at my toil, Or bathe my weary brow? Or shield me when the heavy lash Is raised to give the blow?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thy fond arms press me-and I feel Thy tears upon my cheek; Tears are the only language now, A mother's love can speak.
texts.text: Thy fond arms press me-and I feel Thy tears upon my cheek; Tears are the only language now, A mother's love can speak.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Think of me, mother, as I bend My way across the sea; And midst thy tears, a blessing waft, To her who prays for thee.
texts.text: Think of me, mother, as I bend My way across the sea; And midst thy tears, a blessing waft, To her who prays for thee.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A Prayer
texts.text: A Prayer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Father, we lift the suppliant eye, To where thou reigns't above; We feel that thou canst not deny The children of thy love.
texts.text: Father, we lift the suppliant eye, To where thou reigns't above; We feel that thou canst not deny The children of thy love.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Unshaken faith, unwavering trust, Are all that we can bring; We are thy children, though in dust, To thee we dare to cling.
texts.text: Unshaken faith, unwavering trust, Are all that we can bring; We are thy children, though in dust, To thee we dare to cling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We know that thou wilt not forsake The poor and trembling slave; For him the blessed Saviour spake; And him he came to save.
texts.text: We know that thou wilt not forsake The poor and trembling slave; For him the blessed Saviour spake; And him he came to save.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We feel the chains that bind us all, And bend us to man's will; But can they hold our souls enthrall'd, Or bid our voice be still?
texts.text: We feel the chains that bind us all, And bend us to man's will; But can they hold our souls enthrall'd, Or bid our voice be still?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No:-for thy power is all supreme, Thy word shall yet stand firm; And master and the slave shall e'en To thee for mercy turn.
texts.text: No:-for thy power is all supreme, Thy word shall yet stand firm; And master and the slave shall e'en To thee for mercy turn.
